one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuning a small LLM with PPO and a partial-credit edit-distance reward yields a black-box red-teaming agent that unifies system-prompt and training-data leakage, beats fuzzing/gradient/in-context baselines, and -- uniquely -- keeps working after the PromptGuard guardrail, while its outputs double as safety-alignment data.
key_finding: LeakAgent outperforms rule-based and automated baselines across both leakage tasks and, critically, retains its attack potency under the PromptGuard guardrail where every baseline collapses to zero. On system prompt extraction it achieves the best or near-best average similarity on all six targets (e.g., 0.718 WES on Llama3.1-8B vs 0.252 for PromptFuzz; 0.806 on Mistral-7B; 0.745 on GPT-4o). Against the 12 real GPT Store apps it succeeds on 100% (1.00) both without defense and under PromptGuard, whereas ReAct-Leak/PromptFuzz/Handcraft drop from 0.91/0.83/0.75 to 0.00 once PromptGuard is applied -- the RL agent generates novel prompt strategies the filter has not seen. For the previously-unautomated training-data extraction task it reaches 5.9% ASR on OLMo vs 0.04% (Repeat) and 0.1% (eos). It is also the only attack retaining strength against PromptGuard-filtered Llama3.1-8B (0.589 similarity vs 0.000 for others) and GPT-4o (0.745). Their derived defense LeakAgent-D neutralizes attacks (LeakAgent similarity drops to 0.063) while causing a smaller utility loss than SecAlign, showing the red-teaming data doubles as effective safety-alignment data.
core_contribution: LeakAgent is a black-box red-teaming framework that fine-tunes an open-source LLM (Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct) via deep reinforcement learning (PPO with LoRA/quantization) to act as an attack agent generating natural-sounding adversarial prompts that force a target LLM to leak private information. Unlike prior work, it is unified across two leakage goals -- system prompt extraction AND training-data extraction -- and the authors claim it is the first automated attack for training-data extraction. Three custom designs drive it: (1) a dense reward function based on a Sliding-window Word Edit Similarity (SWES) metric plus a length-matching regularizer (lambda=0.1), designed to give fine-grained partial-credit signal that embedding/ROUGE/BLEU metrics over-reward; (2) a dynamic decoding-temperature schedule (high temperature Thigh for the first k tokens with top-k filtering, base temperature afterward) to force early exploration and reduce reliance on the initial prompt p0; and (3) a diversity regularizer that maintains a pool of high-reward (>0.9) prompts and grants +0.2 reward to newly generated prompts dissimilar from that pool. For the much larger training-data-extraction search space, a two-stage training scheme first does a coarse global search (using the released training set as a searchable database/tool) to find memorized samples, then refines the agent to extract a chosen sample. Contribution is the attack framework; MIA, PLeak, PromptFuzz, Repeat/eos etc. are cited prior work used as baselines, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Black-box, query-only attacker: can query the target LLM but has no access to model internals, weights, gradients, or the training process. Targets are safety-aligned open-source and proprietary chat models (Llama3.1-8B/70B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Claude-3-Haiku). Attack goals: (a) extract an application's hidden system prompt (a risk to LLM-integrated apps such as OpenAI GPT Store), including a harder universal-prompt variant that extracts many system prompts with one adversarial prompt; (b) recover verbatim training data from a model with public training data (OLMo). The attacker must bypass the target's safety alignment and guardrail defenses. On the defense side the authors assume defenders may fine-tune the model or deploy guardrail models (StruQ, SecAlign, PromptGuard).
limitations: Authors are candid that training-data extraction ASR remains low in absolute terms (5.9%), extracting only a small fraction of training tokens, underscoring how hard the task is. RL training is unstable and sensitive to the initial random seed, occasionally failing in some runs due to the ultra-high search space (PPO only partly mitigates variance). Generated training-data-extraction prompts often degenerate into random token combinations rather than coherent prompts. SecAlign resists LeakAgent (0.085 similarity), so a strong training-phase defense already exists for the system-prompt case, and the paper does not find a defense-specific-to-privacy baseline to compare against (it borrows prompt-injection defenses). The GPT Store transferability result relies on human judgment (no ground-truth prompts), and the ablation is run only on system prompt extraction with Llama3.1-8B. Scope excludes training-phase attacks, multimodal models, and LLM-agent targets.

datasets_used: awesome-ChatGPT-prompts (system prompts, split by BERT-embedding clustering into 88 training / 58 testing)
OLMo released training corpus (as searchable database for training-data extraction)
SST-2, SQuAD2.0, GSM8K (utility evaluation via LM Evaluation Harness)
quantitative_results: System prompt extraction WES|ROUGE (Table 1): LeakAgent Llama3.1-8B 0.718|0.716, Llama3.1-70B 0.784|0.730, Mistral-7B 0.806|0.686, GPT-4o 0.745|0.767, GPT-4o-mini 0.640|0.655, Claude-3-Haiku 0.530|0.551
PromptFuzz baseline on Llama3.1-8B: 0.252|0.330 WES|ROUGE (vs LeakAgent 0.718|0.716)

GPT Store attack success rate (Table 2): LeakAgent-GPT4o 1.00 no-defense and 1.00 with PromptGuard; ReAct-Leak 0.91->0.00, PromptFuzz 0.83->0.00, Handcraft 0.75->0.00, PLeak 0.16->0.16 under PromptGuard
Training-data extraction ASR on OLMo (Table 3): LeakAgent 5.9%, Stage-1 0.2%, initial prompt 0.1%, eos 0.1%, Repeat 0.04%

Attacks vs defenses similarity (Table 4): under PromptGuard, LeakAgent 0.589 on Llama3.1-8B-Instruct and 0.745 on GPT-4o vs 0.000 for ReAct-Leak/PromptFuzz; against SecAlign all attacks suppressed (LeakAgent 0.085); against LeakAgent-D LeakAgent 0.063
Reward hyperparameters: SWES sigmoid k=5, x0=0.6; length-regularizer weight lambda=0.1; prompt length sampled from [15,64]; diversity bonus +0.2 for prompts dissimilar from the >0.9-reward pool
Ablation: removing diversity reward (LeakAgent-NoDiv) causes ~10% drop in average similarity; second-stage training reward rises from <0.2 to ~0.7 (Figure 1)
baselines_compared: HandCraft / manual crafting (11 human red-teaming prompts)
